title: The Vatican, Anthropic, and the Future of AI
summary: Pope Leo XIV releases an encyclical on AI. Anthropic was invited. But is this the real story?
imageAlt: Image from source article for What Pope Leo XIV’s First Encyclical Says About the Power of AI
imageCaption: What Pope Leo XIV’s First Encyclical Says About the Power of AI
imageSourceTitle: What Pope Leo XIV’s First Encyclical Says About the Power of AI

body.text: I just read the piece about Pope Leo XIV’s first encyclical, and honestly, it’s a weird place to start looking at technology. It’s not a tech blog post, it’s a religious document, but the subject is the power of AI. It feels like a clash of worlds, but the signal is clear. The Church is trying to set a moral framework for this new infrastructure. It’s a bold move for a leader who usually deals in theology, not algorithms. The timing is particularly interesting because we are already seeing the physical and digital world merge in ways that feel almost surreal.

Why did this show up now? The Vatican invited Anthropic to present on AI. Christopher Olah, a big name in the field, was part of that. It wasn't just a formality; it was a signal that the Vatican sees the technology as a force that needs guidance. They wanted to understand the mechanics before they made the rules. It suggests a partnership between faith and frontier science. The encyclical itself is the first of its kind, which means it sets a precedent for how religious institutions engage with digital tools. It’s a signal that the Vatican isn't just reacting, but is actively shaping the conversation.

This matters because AI isn't just code anymore. It's becoming a platform shift. We're seeing millions of AI agents imperiled by a critical vulnerability in open source packages. We're also seeing the FBI napping people selling deepfakes. The Pope's document is a high-level attempt to address these risks before they scale out of control. It’s not just about safety; it’s about the social contract. If the Vatican is involved, the stakes are higher than just a software update. It touches on the ethics of creation and the responsibility of the creator. The FBI's work on identifying deepfakes shows that the law is catching up, but the Pope is asking a deeper question about intent.

What stands out to me is the sheer scale. From 3D-printable humanoid legs to the Vatican, the infrastructure is everywhere. I don't see this as a policy memo. I see it as a cultural moment. The Pope is trying to say that AI needs to be human. It's a gentle reminder that we can't just push forward without thinking about the people involved. The technology is moving fast, and the world is trying to catch up. It’s a reminder that even the most abstract concepts need a human face. We are building machines that can walk and think, but we need to make sure they don't lose their way.

Tech people care because these stories are usually about leverage. A headline can change how people think about platforms, infrastructure cost, security exposure, product distribution, or the balance of power between vendors and customers.

The nearby context also matters. Around this story, I am seeing related headlines such as Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation. That does not prove one grand narrative, thankfully; news would be suspiciously tidy if it did. But it does show the kinds of questions sitting around the main story.

A few other stories are in the same neighborhood: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation; Millions of AI agents imperiled by critical vulnerability in open source package; FBI agent explains how easy it is to ID people posting AI porn without consent; 3D-printable humanoid legs let robotics experiments run wild. I am treating them as context, not as a marching band. They help explain the mood around the news without stealing the microphone from the main event.
That is why the background is worth slowing down for. A single article may look narrow, but it can still show where pressure is building in the larger system.

The impact is a shift in tone around the category. Sometimes that means more confidence, sometimes more caution, and sometimes just a clearer view of where the hard part of the technology cycle is hiding.
